title: Dog Foreign Body Stuck Between Toes: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Identify foreign bodies in your dog's paws. Learn the urgency levels, symptoms, and why triage photos are essential for your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: A foreign body between a dog's toes is a medium-priority concern that requires veterinary attention within 24 hours to prevent infection and abscesses. Sending a photo to your vet helps them assess the severity and determine if immediate surgery or sedation is needed.

What does it mean if my dog has a foreign body between their toes?

  • A foreign body is any external object, such as grass awns (foxtails), thorns, glass shards, or wood splinters, that becomes lodged in the sensitive skin or webbing of the paw.
  • Because dogs spend so much time on their feet, these objects easily pierce the skin and can migrate deeper into the paw tissue, causing significant pain.

Is a foreign body in my dog's paw considered an emergency?

  • The urgency level is typically classified as Medium; while not immediately life-threatening, it causes significant discomfort and carries a high risk of infection.
  • You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 hours to have the object safely extracted and the area professionally cleaned.

How does providing a photo help the vet triage my dog's condition?

  • A high-resolution photo allows the veterinary team to look for signs of inflammation, discharge, or "tracking" where the object moves under the skin.
  • This visual assessment helps the clinic determine how quickly your dog needs to be seen and whether they might require sedation or minor surgery for removal.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Foreign bodies can become lodged between a dog's toes, resulting in interdigital furunculosis or other complications. Diagnosis involves a thorough examination, potentially under sedation or anesthesia, to identify the foreign material. Radiographs or ultrasound may be necessary to locate all foreign material present. Once identified, the foreign body should be carefully removed.

Chapter: Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 862)

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of something stuck in my dog's paw?
faqs.answer: Common signs include sudden limping, excessive licking or chewing at the paw, swelling between the toes, and a visible draining tract or small hole.

faqs.question: Can I remove the foreign body myself?
faqs.answer: It is generally recommended to have a vet perform the removal. If the object breaks off, pieces left inside can cause chronic infections and may require more invasive surgery later.

faqs.question: What happens if a foxtail is left in the paw?
faqs.answer: Foxtails are particularly dangerous because their barbs allow them to move in only one direction—deeper into the body. They can travel up the leg or into internal organs if not removed promptly.
